Given I am out of position against a deep-stacked and active player, there is no reason to continue with such a weak holding. The pot odds are not sufficient to justify a float here. I will fold.

I’m on the button with Ad2s. With only the blinds posted I can either limp for 100 or open. A2o is a perfectly reasonable button opener: it has the nut‑ace potential and plays well in position. Standard sizing at this table is ~3x the BB, so I’ll raise to 300 total to maximize fold equity against the shortish SB (Flash, 3600) and to take the initiative vs the deep BB (Elon). Plan: if everyone folds I scoop the blinds; if I get one caller I’ll play post‑flop in position and c‑bet favorable textures; if I face a large 3‑bet (roughly >~700–900) I’ll fold given how often A2o is dominated, but I can call small 3‑bets or all‑ins from the short SB for pot odds. Raising to 300 is the +EV line here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Trey of diamonds. Minni has opened for 300 from the Button. While my hand is suited, it is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. Given my relatively short stack of 36 big blinds and the fact that I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position against a large stack, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chips for better opportunities.

Qs8c is a weak offsuit hand, queen-high with no connectivity or suitedness. Facing a 3BB button raise from Minni, who is aggressive but this is a standard fold from BB OOP. Equity vs her wide button range (~40%) is around 38-42%, but postflop playability is poor OOP with no draws. Pot odds 2.25:1 require ~30% equity, but reverse implied odds and position make calling unprofitable long-term. No good 3bet bluff spot here with this hand. Preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 85s. While this is a speculative hand, it is a standard opening candidate from the button to steal the blinds. My stack is relatively short (36 BB), so I want to be aggressive in late positions to pick up chips without a showdown. If I face a 3-bet, I will likely have to fold, but the potential to win the blinds or play a pot in position with a suited hand justifies the raise.

Q6o is a marginal hand at best, offsuit with poor playability and low equity OOP against a button open-raise. Folding is standard to avoid difficult post-flop situations with position disadvantage and deep stacks. Preserve chips for premium spots.

I’m in the big blind with J♥7♣ facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total including my posted blind) — break-even equity ≈ 200/(450+200) ≈ 30.8%. J7o is marginal but defensible from the big blind against a likely wide button opening range, and the price to see a flop is good. 3‑betting OOP with this hand is over‑aggressive (will often get called by better hands and leaves me in awkward spots), while folding is too tight given the pot odds and villain’s frequency. I’ll call the 200 more (300 total) to see a flop and proceed based on texture: continue with pairs or strong draws, fold to heavy action on dry boards.

Check. On Ts-3h-Kc with J♥7♣ I have no pair and no realistic draws — this is a dry, two‑card board that largely hits the button’s opening range (Ks, Ts, Broadway, pockets). There’s no value to bet and thin bluff equity versus a likely wide but often-connected range. I’ll check to control the pot and see a free turn; I’m prepared to fold to a significant bet. I would only continue to a small c‑bet (roughly ≤1/3 pot, ~200) for pot‑odds and to realize showdown equity, but will fold to larger continuation bets to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I opened 8s5s from the button and was called by the Big Blind (Minni). The flop is Ks Ts 3h. While I have no direct pair, I have a backdoor flush draw. This board texture (containing two broadway cards) is generally favorable for my perceived opening range as the pre-flop aggressor. Minni has checked to me, which is standard. I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot to represent a King or a Ten and attempt to take down the pot immediately. If I am called, I can evaluate the turn, especially if a spade appears.

I’m OOP in the big blind with J♥7♣ on T♠-3♥-K♣. Pot is 950 and Flash bets 300; calling 300 requires ~24% equity (300 / (950+300) ≈ 24%). My hand has no pair and very few clean outs (3 jacks → ~12.5% to hit by river), but Flash opened the button and is c-betting a small ~1/3-pot size, which often includes a lot of missed hands/bluffs as well as thin value hands (Kx, Tx, broadways). Given the small bet size and the pot odds, a call to realize equity is correct — the cost is small relative to my 16k stack and I beat a meaningful portion of his c-bet range. I will not raise: J7o is too weak OOP to bluff-raise effectively (I lack fold equity and will get called by better hands or crushed by value), and folding now is too nitty given the price. Plan after the call: if the turn is a blank and Flash checks, I’ll go to showdown; if he barrels small again I may call one more street for pot odds but will fold to large turn/river pressure unless I improve (pair a J). If I hit a jack I will extract value; if Flash raises or jams at any point I will fold unless I’ve improved.

I have J♥7♣ on T♠-3♥-K♣-2♦ and I’m out of position with no pair and essentially no meaningful draws (only ~3 outs to a jack). Flash showed willingness to c‑bet the flop but he hasn’t put more money in on the turn (To Call = 0), so betting OOP as a bluff here is thin — it won’t fold enough of his calling range and will get me in trouble if raised. Checking preserves my stack, lets me see a free river, and keeps pot control. Plan: check; if Flash barrels the river I’ll fold unless I pair up or get very favorable odds to continue.

The turn card is the 2 of diamonds, which is a complete brick. It does not improve my hand (8-high) nor does it pick up any additional equity since no spade appeared. Minni's call on the flop indicates she likely has a piece of the board, such as a Ten, a weak King, or a draw like QJ. Given Minni has shown some stickiness in previous hands (e.g., Hand #2), and my lack of equity, a second barrel here is unlikely to fold out better hands. I will check back to see a free river and potentially represent a trap or evaluate a final bluff on the next street if she checks again.

Check. I have J♥7♣ with no pair and essentially no showdown-improving outs on T♠-3♥-K♣-2♦-2♥. Flash opened and c‑bet the flop then checked the turn and river — his line contains a lot of Kx/Tx and some missed broadways, many of which still beat me. A river bluff has very little fold equity (he can call or shove with ~3k behind), and my prior call/check line isn’t credibly representing trips or a 2, so betting risks a costly raise/call by better hands. The lowest-variance, highest-EV play is to check and see showdown rather than bluff into a range that mostly beats me.

I am on the button with 8-high. On the river (Ts 3h Kc 2d 2h), the board hasn't improved my hand, and I have no showdown value. Minni has checked for the third time (pre-flop, turn, and river), which often indicates a weak made hand like a Ten, a small pocket pair, or a missed draw. Given the pot is 1250 and my stack is 3000, I need to decide if a bluff can force a fold. However, Minni's call on the flop suggests she has some piece of the board. My check on the turn may have capped my range in her eyes, making a river bluff less believable. Since I have 8-high and there's no way to win at showdown, checking back and losing a small pot is safer than risking a significant portion of my stack on a bluff that might get called by a marginal Ten or King.

Preflop on the button in an unraised pot (pot 150, to call 100 for BB). Holding 2c8h, which is a trash hand with poor connectivity, low card strength, offsuit, and minimal potential. No suitedness or wheel potential worth chasing. Deep stacks, better to preserve chips for premium spots rather than speculating with this weak hand out of position postflop against likely calling ranges. Opponents have shown aggression but folding marginal hands pre is standard to maximize EV long-term.

2♦7♣ is the worst offsuit starting hand — zero showdown value and very poor equity. I'm out of position to the big blind, and raising is pointless (it can induce a shove from the 29BB short-stack Flash or commit me with a dominated hand). Calling 50 into a 150 pot gives decent pot odds but playing 2‑7 OOP postflop is a losing proposition long-term. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for better spots.

On the Button with T9o in a 3-handed pot this is a very playable, positionally strong hand. Flash (SB) is short (3k) and Elon (BB) is deep — opening wider from the button is correct to steal blinds/isolate the short stack and to take the initiative postflop. Standard sizing at this table is ~3x the BB; I’ll raise to 300 to apply pressure, avoid giving the BB a cheap flop, and keep my range strong for continuation betting. Plan: if I get called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and probe on later streets; if faced with a large 3-bet from Elon I’ll fold and re-evaluate, if Flash shoves I’ll consider calling depending on shove size and reads (but that decision will be made then).

I am holding 7d 3c in the small blind, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised from the button to 300. Given my relatively short stack of 30BB and being out of position, there is no reason to defend such a marginal hand against a standard open. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Preflop in BB facing a 3x button raise from Minni after SB folds. My KTo offsuit is a strong defending hand vs a wide button range. Minni raises frequently from late position and shows aggression but also calls down light. Pot odds are favorable (200 into 450, ~23% equity needed), and KTo has good playability, blocks strong kings, and decent equity vs her range. Calling keeps the pot small OOP, allows seeing flop cheaply with position disadvantage but deep stacks for postflop maneuvering. Folding too tight, 3-betting unnecessary as it's not premium enough for value and button defends wide.

On this dry rainbow flop of 9c-7s-3h, KTo offsuit hits nothing: no pair, no strong draws, just king-high with backdoor potential. OOP as BB vs aggressive button raiser Minni, who will c-bet wide (Ax, overs, draws, bluffs). Checking is standard to realize equity or fold to aggression without bloating pot. No reason to lead OOP without initiative or made hand. Folding preflop was close but calling was fine vs wide button range; now pot control and folding to likely c-bet maximizes EV long-term vs history of Minni's postflop aggression.

I hold T♥9♦ on a 9♣-7♠-3♥ flop — top pair with a good 10 kicker on a very dry, rainbow board. I opened preflop to 300 and Elon (BB) just called, then checked the flop, so my range includes a lot of overcards, floats and some weak pairs/underpairs; his checked range will fold frequently to a C-bet. I should take the initiative and c-bet for value and fold equity. I’ll size to ~60% pot to extract from worse pairs/weak tens/underpairs and charge any turn draws, while not overcommitting with a single pair vs a potential strong hand. Bet 400 into the 650 pot. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check-raises large I will mostly fold unless the turn gives me two pair/trips or a clear improvement.

On this dry 9c-7s-3h flop, KTo completely misses: king-high with no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw, only weak backdoor potential. Facing a standard c-bet of 400 into 1050 (~38% pot) from aggressive button raiser Minni, who cbets wide vs missed calling range. OOP, calling commits to further tough spots with no equity realization or fold equity, while pot odds (~28% needed post-call) don't justify as villain's range has many pairs, Ax, overs that dominate or semi-bluff. History shows Minni's frequent aggression postflop warrants folding marginal hands like this to preserve stack and avoid spew. Raising is a blatant bluff with poor blockers and position disadvantage. Standard fold maximizes EV.

I am on the button with 4s3s and a stack of 3000 (30 BB). In a 3-handed game, 43s is a speculative but playable opening hand from the button to pressure the blinds. A3o is a marginal hand from the small blind facing a button raise to 3x (300). Minni still to act in BB, but no need to get involved with this hand when stacks are deep (except Flash at 27BB). Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in this long 150-hand game.

I’m in the BB with 5♦2♠ facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call (I’d need ~31% equity: 200/(450+200)), but 5-2o is severely dominated by a button’s opening range and has almost no post‑flop playability out of position. A light 3‑bet to 500 is feasible sometimes, but with this garbage hand it’s a thin bluff against a 27bb opener who can call or shove — not profitable long term. Preserve stack and fold to this open.

Preflop on the button with Jd6h offsuit. Table history shows loose play and frequent preflop action, but button position allows postflop control. Raising small to ~2.5BB builds pot while risking little relative to stacks. Minraise is 200, but sizing to 250 pressures blinds effectively. EV positive assuming reasonable fold equity.

Elon opened to 250 from the button in a 3‑handed pot. I have A♦3♣ in the small blind — a marginal off‑suit Ace that blocks some of his Ace combinations but is easily dominated by stronger A‑kickers. Folding is too nitty here vs a wide BTN range; 3‑betting is unattractive because I’d be bloating a pot out of position with a weak kicker and Flash (BB) is short (~30bb) and could isolate or shove, putting me in a tough spot. I’ll defend and keep the pot manageable by calling the extra 200 (total 250). Postflop plan: on A‑high boards play for value and protection; on dry boards check and fold to serious aggression; if the BB jams over my call I’ll fold most of the time (A3o has poor equity vs a short‑stack shove). Calling preserves flexibility and exploits the BTN’s wide opening range.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. Elon opened for 2.5x from the Button and Minni called from the Small Blind. The price to call is only 150 more into a pot that will be 750. Ace-high has reasonable equity against a Button opening range, and getting roughly 5:1 odds makes this a mandatory call to see a flop. While a 3-bet is an option, given my relatively short stack of 3050 compared to the blinds (30.5 BB), keeping the pot manageable and realizing equity is a solid approach here.

I have A♦3♣ on a 5♠ Q♠ Q♣ flop in a 3‑way pot after calling preflop. I’m out of position and the board is paired with two spades — BTN’s opening range contains a lot of Qx and pocket pairs, and the short BB can have spade draws or Qx as well. C‑betting multi‑way is low EV: I’ll be called by better hands and only fold out air. I’ll check to control the pot and avoid bloating it with a marginal Ace‑high. If I face a small probe (roughly ≤1/3 pot) I’ll call to realize equity against draws/bluffs; I’ll fold to larger bets or any shove since my hand has poor showdown value.

On this 5s-Qs-Qc flop, Minni checked to me. As the Big Blind, I have Ace-high which has some showdown value on this paired board. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor on the Button, is still to act behind me. In a multi-way pot, checking is the most standard play with my hand. I want to see how Elon reacts to this board. If both players check, I may have the best hand, or I can potentially improve on the turn. There is no need to bet here into two opponents on a board that is likely to have hit the Button's range or where the Small Blind might be trapping with a Queen.

Flop of Qs Qc 5s is very coordinated with top pair possible, flush draw, and straight draws. I raised preflop from button with Jd6h, which completely misses, giving me pure air with no pair, draw, or showdown value. Both opponents checked, which is standard but could represent weakness from blinds' wide calling ranges. As the preflop aggressor, a continuation bet maintains my range advantage—button opens are perceived stronger than SB/BB calls. Betting denies equity to any spade draws, gutshots, or weaker pairs that might have called pre. Fold equity is high here since villains often check-fold marginal hands postflop (seen in prior hands like Hand 15, 23). Flash is shortest at ~29BB (2900), so sizing to ~2/3 pot pressures him specifically while keeping Minni's deep stack in mind. Bet 500 (2/3 pot) balances value from trapped pairs/worse queens and bluffs out draws. Calling/raising unlikely given board and my position. Check-fold too passive, check-call terrible with no hand.

Board: 5♠ Q♠ Q♣. I have A♦3♣ in the SB and I checked earlier to control the pot. Elon (BTN) bets 500 into a 1250 pot (~40% pot). Calling 500 gives me ~28.6% pot odds (500 / (1250+500)), and vs a wide BTN c-bet range that includes many bluffs and spade/straight draws my ace-high often has enough equity to justify a call. Raising OOP is unattractive: I have a weak kicker, no spade blocker, and the short-stacked BB (≈29bb) acts after me and can shove, which would put me in a very uncomfortable spot with A3o. Plan: call to keep the pot manageable and realize equity; fold to a large raise/jam from Flash or to very strong recovery aggression from Elon. If the turn pairs or brings an A I'll proceed for value; otherwise remain cautious and fold to heavy action.

On this flop of 5s-Qs-Qc, Elon has bet 500 into a pot of 750 (66% pot) after both Minni and I checked. Minni has already called the 500. This multi-way action makes the situation difficult. While I have Ace-high (Ac7s) with the Ace of clubs, the board is heavily paired and Minni calling suggests she has either a Queen, a pocket pair, or a flush draw. However, given the pot odds (calling 500 to win a total pot of 2250), I'm getting 4.5:1. With my Ace-high, I might still have the best hand if Elon is continuation betting with air, though Minni's call is concerning. I will call one street to see if the turn improves my hand or if the action slows down, but I must be ready to fold to further aggression.

Turn K♣ pairs the board (5♠ Q♠ Q♣ K♣). I hold A♦3♣ — a weak Ace‑high with virtually no way to improve to a winning five‑card hand (no realistic flush/straight outs). Both opponents called Elon’s flop c‑bet, so their ranges are weighted to Qx, Kx, pairs/sets and spade draws; my hand is behind most of that range. Per my earlier plan I should remain cautious when the turn is not an A or a blank that helps my equity. Betting OOP in a 3‑way pot with a marginal hand is low EV — I’ll get called by better hands and only fold out the air occasionally. I’ll check to control the pot and avoid bloating it; I will fold to significant turn aggression (especially a shove from short‑stack Flash) and only consider calling a very small probe if the price is correct to realize equity. If checked to the river I’ll re‑evaluate based on the river card (bet for value on an A, otherwise proceed cautiously).

On the turn, the King of Clubs is a scare card that likely favors the pre-flop aggressor's (Elon) range. Having only Ace-high and being out of position relative to the Button in a multi-way pot, I have no reason to bet. My hand has very little showdown value now that another overcard has arrived, and Minni's check-call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board or a draw. Checking is the most disciplined play to see how the action develops behind me.

Turn board Kc brings another high card and club flush draw possibilities, making the board even more coordinated (paired queens, spade flush draw, club draw, straight draws like JT). My Jd6h remains pure air with no pair, no draw, backdoor nothing relevant. Double-barrel bluff maintains range balance, exploits fold equity against likely 5x, weak Qx, or missed draws that called flop but dislike the turn. Flash critically short at 2400 (~24BB effective), so sizing bet to ~2/3 pot pressures him maximally without overbetting vs Minni's deep stack. Bet 1500 denies equity to any remaining draws (spade/club flush, gutshots) and gets value from worse if called light. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or facing river aggression; folding not applicable. Pot commitment low relative to stack (18925), positive EV bluff given position, history, and dynamics.

Board is 5♠ Q♠ Q♣ K♣; I hold A♦3♣. Elon (BTN) bets 1500 into a pot of 3750 — I must call 1500 (pot odds ≈ 1500 / (3750+1500) = 28.6%). My hand has essentially three clean outs (the remaining Aces) and is behind most of Elon’s likely continuing range (Qx, Kx, pairs) and behind many of Flash’s calling hands. Versus a range weighted to Qx/Kx/pairs my river equity is well under the ~28.6% needed. Additionally Flash is a short stack (~2400) and can shove over my call, which would put me in a very difficult spot with A3o. Raising OOP with a weak Ace and a poor kicker is low EV and will get called or isolated by better hands. Per my earlier plan to check and fold to significant turn aggression, the correct, least costly line is to fold now and preserve stack for better spots.

On this turn board of 5s-Qs-Qc-Kc, I am holding only Ace-high. Elon has fired a second barrel of 1500 into a pot of 2250 after both Minni and I checked. Minni, who had called the flop bet, has already folded. Elon's bet into two players on a board with a pair of Queens and a King is very strong, likely representing a Queen, a King, or better. My Ace-high has almost no equity against his value range, and calling would commit more than half of my remaining stack (2400). Given the board texture and the betting action, this is a clear fold.

I'm on the button with 9s6s — a suited connector with good playability in position. It's 3-handed (SB Flash ~2350 short, BB Elon very deep). The table's standard open size has been ~3x the BB, so raising to 300 (total) is the best option: it applies pressure to the short-stacked SB (who often folds or shoves), leverages my positional advantage, and denies the BB free or cheap flops. If Flash shoves I can call (I cover him and 9s6s has reasonable equity vs a wide shove range). If Elon 3-bets big I'll fold and preserve my stack. Plan postflop: play aggressively in position, c-bet favorable textures, and fold to heavy resistance when out of position against large sizing.

I am in the Small Blind with Td 4s, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised from the Button, and I have a relatively short stack of 2350 chips compared to the blinds (50/100). Playing this hand out of position against the chip leader is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Pocket 5s (55) in the Big Blind facing a button raise to 300 (3BB). This is a standard defending spot for a small pocket pair against a wide button opening range. Minni's history shows frequent preflop raises from various positions, often with marginal hands, suggesting a loose range. Folding is too tight given pot odds (calling 200 into 450, ~30% needed but postflop potential much higher). 3-betting OOP risks bloating the pot without strong postflop playability if missed. Calling keeps the pot small, allows set-mining or flopping two-pair/overpair, and maintains fold equity postflop if needed. Will play cautiously postflop OOP, c-betting flops that hit my range or checking floats.

Flop completely missed with 55: underpair to Q-T-7 rainbow board, no draws. OOP heads-up vs button raiser with wide range. Board connects decently with villain's range (Qx, Tx, suited connectors, pairs). Donk betting OOP is suboptimal - poor playability if called/raised, blocks own checking range. Check standard for pot control and to realize equity via cheap calls or fold equity. Plan: vs small cbet (e.g., <400), call once to pot odds (~30% equity needed, 55 has ~35-45% vs button range); fold to larger/aggression. Deep stacks but no set-mining value now since missed. Prior hands show Minni c-bets flops actively but can check weak hands. Goal: minimize losses, avoid spew.

I was the preflop aggressor and Elon checked the flop (7s Tc Qh). With 9s6s I have no pair — just weak backdoor spade and straight possibilities — so my hand has little intrinsic showdown value. As the raiser in position this is a textbook spot to continuation-bet to leverage range advantage and fold out unpaired hands/weak floats. Plan: take it down if he folds; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn (check blanks, fold to heavy aggression, only continue when I pick up real equity or a favorable card). If he check‑raises large I’ll let this go — I don’t have the equity to defend vs big aggression.

Facing a small continuation bet of 325 (~33% pot) into 975 on a Q-T-7 rainbow board as OOP with 55. Board is somewhat coordinated but dry, hitting villain's button range (Qx, Tx, suited connectors) while 55 blocks sets and has showdown value as underpair. Equity ~35-45% vs typical wide cbet range including bluffs, Ax, K-high, missed draws which we beat or race. Pot odds excellent: 325 to call into 1300 (~25% equity needed). Deep effective stacks (158BB) allow implied odds if we improve to sets/overpairs. Folding too tight given history of Minni's loose-aggressive play and frequent cbets; raising OOP turns decent equity hand into semi-bluff with poor playability if called. Standard call to realize equity, control pot size, and re-evaluate turn (fold to strong barrel, call/raise if improves or board helps). Consistent with preflop plan for set-mining defense.

Pocket 5s OOP first to act on turn board 7s Tc Qh Ah (pot 1300, eff stacks deep ~158BB). Turn Ace of hearts brings top pair for Ax, completes heart flush draws, adds straight draw potential (KJ, J9). My underpair now faces stronger range: villain's button open + flop cbet likely includes Ax (improved), Qx/Tx (TP), pairs, draws, some bluffs. 55 equity ~30-40% vs polarized range but poor playability OOP - beats bluffs/missed draws but crushed by value. Donk betting blocks checks, poor SPR control, invites raises. Check standard: induces bluffs from missed draws, allows pot control. Plan: fold to bet (even small as reverse implied odds low, board favors villain); check-behind rare weak hands uncalled. Minimizes losses long-term vs aggressive Minni (history: frequent cbets, loose opens, but folds to 3bets/raises). Consistent defensive strategy after flop float.

Check. I missed the flop and the Ace on the turn only makes life harder — my 9s6s has essentially no showdown value and the backdoor spade plan is dead now that the turn is Ah. I c‑bet the flop (325 into 650) and Elon called, which tells me he has at least a decent piece of the flop (Qx, Tx, a pair, or a draw) and the Ace likely improved a lot of hands in his calling range (AQ, A‑x, Qx that now plays well). Betting the turn as a pure bluff would require folding out a lot of hands that are very willing to call (top pair, second pair, any Ace), so my fold equity is small and I’d be risking more of my deep stack with almost zero equity. Checking controls the pot (pot = 1,300), keeps options for river bluff or a cheap showdown if I pick up a pair, and allows me to fold to real aggression. Plan: check‑fold to a sizable bet; if he checks back I’ll reassess the river (look to bluff only on a favorable blank or continue for thin value if I pair).

River 5d perfectly disguises my pocket 5s into top set, crushing villain's likely range. Board: 7s Tc Qh Ah 5d – no flush completes (two hearts max), straight draws (e.g., KJ/J9 for broadway-ish, but unlikely given line) whiff. Minni's line (button minraise, small flop cbet on dry Q-high, check turn A bringing OESD/flushdraw completion) caps her at Qx (QJ/QT), Ax (slowplayed or weak kicker), Tx, mid-pairs, bluffs/missed draws – very few sets (77/TT/QQ/AA) given check-back turn. History shows Minni loose-aggressive pre/flop but calls value postflop (e.g., Hand 2 river call 1675, Hand 17 multi-street calls). Deep stacks (~150BB eff), OOP first act: bet extracts max value from TP/TK/overpairs/draws while denying cheap showdown. Check risks villain check-behind weak or small bet uncalled. Size 900 (~70% pot) targets 1-2 streets value, thin value from worse, fold-out pure air; avoids overbet folding Qx/Ax or getting raised off equity (rare). Consistent with defensive preflop/float, now monster realization.

Elon called my flop c‑bet and both of us checked the Ace on the turn — that line strongly indicates a made piece of the board (Qx, Tx) or an Ax/medium pair that is content to control the pot. His river bet of 900 into 1,300 (pot 2,200 after the bet) prices a call at ~41% equity (900 to win 2,200). My hand (9s6s) has essentially no showdown value on the river — I only beat pure air/bluffs — and I have no blocker that meaningfully reduces his value combinations. Raising as a bluff to 1,800 (min‑raise) is too high-variance here against a very deep, calling opponent and is unlikely to fold enough better hands to be +EV. Given the line, sizing and my hand strength, calling requires implausibly high bluff frequency from Elon, so the correct play is to fold and preserve the healthy 15,800 stack for better, more favorable spots.

I am on the button with 5-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. With only 2,350 chips compared to the blinds' much larger stacks, I need to be more selective with the hands I play to avoid being bled out. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Qh8d is a marginal hand from the SB facing a fold from the button. With the BB likely defending wide but fold equity available, a small raise attempts to steal the 150 dead money. Post-flop OOP, but Q8o has decent playability for top pair or draws. Deep stacks allow maneuvering. Raising 3x the BB (300 total) balances aggression and pot control. Folding too tight in SB position; calling OOP realization poor long-term.

I have J♣4♥ in the big blind facing a 3bb open to 300 from Elon (SB). Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call, so I need roughly 33% equity to break even. J4o is a poorly performing, largely dominated hand vs a standard SB opening range and plays badly out of position post‑flop. A tiny raise to the shown min (500) won’t generate reliable fold equity and a larger 3‑bet is too risky with this holding against a deep, often-aggressive opponent. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

3-handed on the button with K5o in an unraised pot (assuming SB limped or folded, BB checked). K5o is a profitable raising hand from the button due to position, king blocker, and fold equity against blinds who have folded frequently in history. Flash is short-stacked at 2250, increasing fold likelihood. Raising standard 3x BB to 300 builds pot or takes it down immediately. Calling is passive and gives up initiative; folding too tight in this spot. Pot odds favorable post-raise if contested.

Elon opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the small blind with Qc8h — a marginal offsuit holding that fares poorly out of position and is often dominated by a button open. Calling would cost me 250 more into a 450 pot (about 35–36% equity required), which Q8o doesn’t have vs a typical BTN range, and a tiny raise to 500 is too thin — it invites action from the deep-stacked opener or a shove/call shove from the short-stacked big blind (Flash, 2250). I’ll conserve chips and avoid playing a dominated, OOP spot with a marginal hand. Fold and wait for a better opportunity in position.

I am holding 2s4c in the big blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Elon has raised from the button, and while button ranges are generally wide, this hand has very poor playability and equity. With a stack of 22.5 big blinds, I need to be more selective about the hands I defend or shove with. Folding is the most prudent play here.

Three-handed on the button I should be opening a wide range. Q♠8♦ is marginal but playable in position — it has a high-card blocker (Q) and decent postflop playability. Limping hands here just gives the blinds good odds and invites multiway pots; I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the short-stacked SB (Flash ≈22bb) and the deep BB (Elon). A standard 3xBB open (300 total) is the right balance: it maximizes fold equity, keeps the pot manageable, and still lets me call a short-stack shove if Flash jams. If Elon 3-bets large out of position I can fold and avoid a marginal spot. Raise to 300 to steal the blinds and play position postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 6c, which is a marginal suited hand. Minni, on the button, has opened for 300. While suited connectors or gappers are sometimes playable, my stack is relatively short at 22 BB (2200 chips) compared to the blinds (50/100). Calling out of position against a big stack like Minni is generally unfavorable, and 3-betting light with this hand into Elon's big blind is risky given my stack depth. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my remaining chips for a better spot.

J♥3♦ is a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, domination risk, and terrible postflop playability. Minni's button raise to 300 (3x BB) is standard with a wide range. Pot odds are 200 to call into 450 (pot odds ~3.25:1, requiring ~23% equity), but J3o has far less than that against a typical button opening range (~35-40% of hands). No position advantage postflop, deep stacks (220 BB effective). Folding preserves stack for premium spots. History shows Minni aggressive but no need to defend trash here.

I have been dealt a pocket pair (6s 6d) on the button. With a stack of 2200 (22 big blinds), I am relatively short-stacked compared to the other two players. Pocket sixes are a strong opening hand, especially in a three-handed game. My goal is to either win the pot pre-flop or see a low-card flop where my pair might be best. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300.

Preflop in small blind facing a button raise to 300 (3x BB). My hand is 4c9d, which is a complete trash hand with no high card value, no suitedness, no straight potential, and poor playability out of position. Folding is straightforward as it has zero equity against a typical button opening range and commits me to a tough spot postflop OOP with a tiny stack commitment relative to pot but no hand strength. Preserving stack for better spots over 150 hands.

Call to 300. A♦5♦ is a strong defending hand heads-up versus a button open: it has good equity (ace blocker + suited) and plays well postflop. Calling 200 more into a 450 pot gives ~30% break-even odds, which A5s exceeds against a wide button raising range. I don't want to min-3-bet to 500 because Flash only has ~19BB and that small 3-bet will just induce a shove or a flip where my weak kicker can cost chips. By calling I keep the pot manageable, realize equity, and retain flexibility postflop: be aggressive if I hit (ace/flush/strong draws), otherwise pot-control or fold to heavy action.

I have Ad5d on 8d–Qh–Td: nut-flush draw + an ace blocker and an ace-high. This is a premium semi-bluff — strong equity to realize and good fold equity versus Flash’s wide preflop raising range. Pot is 650; I’ll take the initiative and bet to charge pairs and weaker draws while denying equity to non-diamond hands. I size ~60% pot (400): big enough to fold out many marginal made hands and to build the pot when I hit, but small enough that I’m not overly committed if he fights back. If he calls I can continue depending on the turn (bet big for value on a diamond or an ace, pot-control or check-call on blanks); if he shoves I’m prepared to call given my ~35% to make the flush plus ace pair outs.

On this flop of 8d-Qh-Td, my pocket sixes have become an underpair. This board texture is very favorable for Minni's calling range from the big blind, containing many queens, tens, eights, and draws. Minni's lead for 400 into 650 (a donk bet) signifies strength or a strong draw. With only two outs to improve and many dangerous turn cards possible, I will fold to preserve my remaining 1600 chips for a better situation.

Preflop on the button in an unraised pot (pot 150, to call 100, implying SB folded and BB checked). My hand 3s6d is extremely weak: offsuit, low cards, poor connectivity (3-gapper), minimal showdown value or draw potential. Even 3-handed on button vs short-stacked BB (18BB), this is not in a profitable opening range due to low equity when facing a shove (~25-35% vs wide shoving range) and terrible postflop playability if limped/called. Folding preserves chips for premium spots in a long 150-hand session.

I’m in the small blind with As4s (suited ace). Pot is 150 and it’s heads-up vs the big blind (Flash) who is a short stack (~18bb). Out of position, I don’t want to limp and give free equity — a standard aggressive open from the SB is best. Raise to 200 (2bb) to seize the initiative, apply fold equity against the BB’s wide range, and keep the pot manageable versus a short stack who may shove or call. As4s has good playability postflop and reasonable equity vs a shove range, so a small raise to 200 maximizes long‑term EV here.

I am in the big blind holding 8d 8s. The small blind, Minni, has raised to 200, making it 100 more for me to call. My stack is quite short (1800 chips), while the others have significantly more. Pocket eights are a strong hand three-handed. I could call to see a flop, but given my stack size, I want to take control of the pot or force a fold now. A 3-bet here defines my hand and puts pressure on a wide small-blind opening range. Raising to 600 total leaves me enough behind to maneuver on many flops or commit if necessary.

Flash (BB) is a short stack (~1,300, ~13bb) and has 3‑bet to 600 after my 200 open. Pot = 800, I must put in 400 more to call — break‑even equity = 400/(800+400) = 33.3%. If he shoves the rest after I call (adds ~700), my needed equity to call that shove would be ~26.9% (700/2600). As4s is a strong candidate vs a short‑stack 3‑bet/shove range (suited ace, decent blockers and reasonable equity), so calling is +EV. Reraising to 1000 or jamming is unnecessary here: it commits more into a short stack that is likely to shove or fold and removes the option to see a flop cheaply; folding is too tight. I’ll call the 400 more to make my total 600 and re-evaluate on the flop (or call a shove, since pot odds justify it).

Flop: Qh 9s Ks. I hold As4s — the nut spade draw (two spades in my hand + two on board) with an ace blocker. Flash is a very short stack who 3‑bet preflop, so his range is wide (broadways, K/Qx, some pockets, suited connectors and some Ax). Pot is 1,200 and I act first. Checking surrenders a lot of fold equity and lets many hands I can beat see a free turn; betting pressures his wide range and folds out many K/Qx and medium pairs while getting value from worse draws and weaker pairs that might call. If he shoves I only need ~27% equity to call profitably (his shove is ~700 effective), and my nut flush draw + ace blocker gives me well above that vs a typical shove range. I’ll apply pressure but keep the size reasonable so I don’t overcommit if he flats — betting ~2/3 pot (800) maximizes folds and still leaves me the option to call a shove with good equity or continue post‑call if he just calls. Plan: bet 800; call any shove, and re-evaluate if he merely calls.

The flop of K-Q-9 is extremely unfavorable for my pocket eights, as there are three overcards and the board is highly coordinated. Minni's lead of 800 into a 1200 pot represents significant strength, likely at least a pair of Queens or Kings, or a strong draw like JT. With only 1300 chips remaining, calling 800 would leave me with only 500 chips (less than half a pot-sized bet) on the turn, essentially committing me to a hand where I have very little equity. Folding preserves my remaining stack to find a better spot.

On the button with 4♦3♠ — a weak offsuit hand — but in 3-handed play position is powerful and the small blind is short (Flash ~1,250 ≈ 12.5bb). Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop. I want to apply pressure and pick up the blinds rather than passively limp and give the BB a free look. I’ll open-raise to 300 (3×BB): this sizing is standard at this table, gives good fold equity versus the short SB, and keeps the pot manageable with a marginal holding. If Flash shoves all-in (~1,250) I’ll fold these cards (dominated/poor equity). If called, I’ll use positional advantage to c-bet or disengage depending on the flop texture and opponent tendencies.

I have a pocket pair (66) and a very short stack of 1,250 chips (12.5 BB). In a 3-handed game, 66 is significantly ahead of a standard button opening range. Given my stack size, calling leaves me with less than 10 BB and puts me in a difficult position post-flop. Moving all-in is the optimal play here to maximize fold equity and give myself a chance to double up and stay in the game.

T8o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability and no strong potential draws. Facing a button raise to 3BB and a large 3-bet to 12.5BB from the SB (Flash), who appears short-stacked now with only 50 chips left after the raise, indicating strength or desperation. I'm OOP in BB, pot odds are approximately 1.4:1 (1150 to call into 2800), insufficient for this speculative hand multiway. Better to fold and wait for better spots to maximize stack long-term.

I opened to 300 on the button with 4♦3♠ and Flash (SB) shoved/raised to 1,250 total. Calling costs me 950 into a 1,650 pot, so I need ~36.5% equity to break even. 4‑3 offsuit has poor equity against a short‑stack shove range (many pairs, A‑x and broadways that dominate), so it’s well below the required equity. Raising further is pointless since Flash is effectively all‑in and any extra creates a side pot he can’t contest. With a deep stack and many better spots to apply pressure, I fold and preserve chips.

I am on the button with 3h 4s, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. My current stack is 1,700 chips (17 big blinds), making me the short stack at the table. While the button is a good position to steal blinds, 4-3 offsuit lacks the equity to defend against a 3-bet, and both Elon and Minni have shown a willingness to play back or defend. Given the quality of the hand, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my remaining chips for a better opportunity.

Q3o is a trash hand from the small blind in an unopened pot (Button folded). It has poor post-flop playability, low equity against BB's calling range (likely wide but still dominates this), and is easily dominated by better Qx or pairs. Position is terrible post-flop. Folding loses only the SB 50 chips, which is negligible over 150 hands with deep stacks. Wait for better spots to maximize EV.

AKo is a premium hand, especially on the button with the small blind likely folded (pot 150, to-call 100 aligns with blinds posted and SB out). Facing a short-stacked big blind (16BB), a standard 3x open-raise builds the pot, has great equity against calling or shoving ranges, and applies pressure. Raising to 300 is optimal sizing for position and stack dynamics.

Facing a button open to 300 from the small blind with 7♠5♥ (offsuit). I’ve already got 50 in, but calling 250 more puts me OOP against a wide but mostly stronger opening range. The hand is weak and plays poorly postflop; a raise to 500 would be a thin bluff that can be punished by a 3-bet or a BB shove (BB is short stacked at 1,600), and calling gives poor odds and often leaves me in tough multiway spots. Long-run EV is to conserve chips in this spot and wait for a better hand/position. Fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Five offsuit. Elon, on the Button, has raised to 300. This is a 3x open. Given my short stack of 1600 chips (16 big blinds), I have very little room to navigate post-flop with a weak hand like J5o. Calling would leave me with 1300 chips and out of position against a player with a massive stack. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my remaining stack for a better opportunity to double up.

With three players, my opening range should be wide — 10♠6♠ is fine to raise on the button. I want to take the initiative, steal the blinds, and play postflop in position. Flash (SB) is short (~15.5bb) and often will fold or shove; a raise to 300 (3bb) gives good fold equity and lets me isolate him or play heads-up vs Elon. If Flash jams I’ll be willing to call given the pot odds and equity vs a short-stack shove; if Elon 3-bets large I’ll fold and avoid a dominated spot out of position. Raise to 300 to apply pressure and maximize long-term EV.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 2d suited and a stack of 1550 chips (15.5 BB). Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. 3d 2d is a very weak hand to play out of position, even given its suitedness, especially with such a short stack. I should preserve my chips for a better doubling-up opportunity.

KsQs is a premium suited broadway hand from the big blind facing a button raise. Button ranges are wide, and KQs dominates hands like KJo, AJo, suited broadways, connectors. SB folded (short stack anyway), so likely heads-up vs Minni. 3-betting for value, initiative, and to build the pot OOP with a strong hand that plays well postflop. Sizing to 900 (3x the raise) is standard and balanced. Deep stacks allow for it. Folding or calling would be too passive.

Call — commit a total of 900. Facing Elon's 3‑bet to 900 after my 300 button open, T♠6♠ is marginal but very playable in position with deep stacks (I have 16,150 vs his 21,050). The pot is 1,250 and calling 600 more requires ~32% equity; T6s has suited/straight potential and good postflop playability. Elon’s 3‑bet size is a standard 3x and will contain both value and some bluffs, so folding here is too passive. A 4‑bet bluff to 1,500 with T6s is thin — I lack the necessary blockers and would unnecessarily bloat the pot. Plan: call to see a flop in position, continue aggressively when I connect (pair, spade or straight draws), keep pot control on dry boards, and fold to heavy turn/river pressure when I have no equity. If Elon shows extreme strength (large 4‑bet shove), I will re‑evaluate and likely fold.

Flop Kh As 3c is a good hit for my 3-bet range: top pair with Queen kicker on an Ace-high rainbow board. I have initiative from the 3-bet, acting first OOP vs Minni's calling range (wide button 3-bet call: Ax, Kx, suited broadways, connectors). This board favors my range. C-bet for thin value vs worse pairs (KQ/AQ worse, mid Kx), protection vs gutshots (e.g., JT, QJ), and to deny equity to floats/draws. Checking risks giving free cards to Ax draws or allowing Minni to bluff/rep AK/Ax. Pot 1850, bet ~65% pot (1200) balances value and sets up turns well with deep stacks (21k/15k). Minni loose from history, likely calls lighter.

Elon 3‑bet me preflop, so his continuing range on Kh As 3c is heavily weighted to Ax/Kx, overpairs and sets — hands that have me crushed. I hold T♠6♠ which missed this flop entirely: no pair and only a remote backdoor flush (would need two spades on turn+river, ~4% by river). Pot math: calling 1,200 into a 3,050 pot (making it ~4,250) requires ~28% equity; my hand has far less versus his polarized 3‑bet/CB range. A raise as a bluff is too thin (I don’t block enough of his strong combos) and will get me exploited or commit too much out of position if he fights back. Fold and preserve my deep stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 6s4h. My stack is very low at 1550 chips, while my opponents have over 15k and 22k. With only 15.5 big blinds, I need to be extremely selective with my entries. 6-high offsuit is one of the weakest hands in the game and does not play well post-flop, especially against two large stacks who have shown aggression in previous hands. Folding is the correct move to preserve my remaining stack for a better opportunity.

9s5d is a trash hand: offsuit, disconnected, low cards with poor playability and equity even heads-up against BB range 3-handed. Button (Flash) folded, so facing only BB (Minni). Standard play is to fold from SB with this holding to avoid difficult post-flop situations OOP with a weak hand. Preserves stack for better spots in this deep-stacked cash game aiming to maximize over 150 hands.

A5s is a strong suited Ace hand from the button position, with excellent playability, flush and straight potential, and nut flush blocker. Facing a small to-call of 100 into a 150 pot (great implied odds even for a call), but raising is optimal to take initiative, isolate the likely wide SB range (Minni opens frequently from history), build the pot when ahead, and apply fold equity against the short-stacked BB (Flash has 1450, ~14BB, but unlikely premium). Position is huge postflop. Raising to 400 total (4x the effective open, minraise-aware) sizes well for value/protection without overcommitting deep stacks. Folding or flat-calling weaker here long-term.

Call 400 total (350 more). Pocket sixes are a hand I want to see a flop with here — they have decent equity vs a wide BTN open and huge implied odds if I hit a set. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3-bet (the min 3-bet is too small to generate reliable fold equity and a larger 3-bet just builds a big OOP pot). Flash (BB) is short (~14–15bb) and will often fold to a 3-bet or shove; calling preserves flexibility (if Flash jams I’ll re-evaluate but 66 fares well vs a short-shove range). Folding is too tight given my hand and stack. Plan: call, see the flop, and proceed based on texture — look to set-mine or pot-control OOP, fold to heavy aggression if I miss and the board becomes hostile.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 5c, a suited connector. The stack sizes are quite lopsided, as I only have 14.5 big blinds left (1450 chips). Elon raised to 400 from the button, and Minni called from the small blind. The pot is currently 900, and it costs me 300 to call. While my hand has some post-flop potential, my short stack puts me in a 'push or fold' situation or at least makes calling very risky because I won't have much leverage post-flop. However, getting 4:1 odds on a call with a suited connector is mathematically tempting. But given I only have 1450 chips total, calling 300 leaves me with 1150 on the flop in a 1200 pot. This is essentially an all-in situation if I hit anything. Given the pot odds and the playability of suited connectors, calling is acceptable, but I must be prepared to commit on any favorable flop.

Check. I called preflop to set-mine and this flop (T♣ K♥ 9♦) is hostile to an underpair — two overcards and no meaningful backdoor draws. Out of position I want pot control and to see how the short-stacked BB and the button act before bloating the pot. Plan: check and evaluate — call a small c‑bet to realize equity, fold to a large raise/3‑bet from Elon, but call a short-stack jam from Flash (~1,150) because the pot odds require only ~33% equity and his shove will be very wide. If I pair up or hit a set on the turn I'll transition to extracting value; otherwise keep the pot small.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 5c. The flop is Tc-Kh-9d, which provides me with no pairs and no straight draws (only a very remote runner-runner flush or straight possibility). The pot is 1200, and I have only 1150 chips remaining. Minni has checked. Since I have completely missed this flop and have almost no equity against two opponents on this board, I should check and hope for a free card. If anyone bets, I will be forced to fold as I cannot justify committing my final chips with such a weak hand.

Preflop button raise with A5s gets called by SB (Minni, wide defender from history) and short BB (Flash ~11BB). Flop Tc Kh 9d is rainbow, somewhat drawy (JQ, Q8 str8 draws), but favors my aggressive button range (KK+, AK, AQ, ATs+, broadways) over callers' capped ranges. A5s blocks AA/AK, has ace-high SDV, backdoor flush/straight potential. Both check, so c-bet for thin value from Ax worse (AJo+), overcards, pairs; protection vs draws; fold equity vs short BB shove-all-in range. Size 700 (~60% pot) balances value/protection without folding out too much, deep vs Minni but exploits Flash's stack pressure. Checking risks giving free cards to draws/improvers while losing initiative OOP.

Call 700. I called preflop to set-mine, and this flop (T♣ K♥ 9♦) is hostile to an underpair — out of position I want pot control and to realize equity. Elon’s continuation bet of 700 is small relative to the pot so calling gives ~27% required equity (call 700 to win ~2600) and is the right play to see a turn. Raising OOP with 66 here has little fold equity and just bloats the pot vs likely K/T/9 and broadway holdings. If the short-stacked BB (Flash ~1,150) jams, I will call the shove because his range will be very wide and the pot odds are favourable for a set-mine; if Elon raises significantly (min-raise to ~1,400 or larger) I will respect that heavy action and fold. If I hit a six or improve on the turn I’ll look to extract value; otherwise keep the pot controlled.

I have 7c 5c on a Tc Kh 9d board. I have no pair and no real draw. Elon bet 700 and Minni called. While the pot odds are mathematically attractive (nearly 4:1), my hand has virtually zero equity on this highly coordinated board against two opponents who have already shown strength. With only 1150 chips remaining, calling 700 would leave me with only 450 chips and no realistic way to win the pot. Saving my remaining 11.5 big blinds for a better spot is more prudent than've calling off most of my stack here.

I have 6♣6♠ on T♣ K♥ 9♦ 2♦ — an underpair with no diamond and only two outs to make a set on the river (~4.3%). I called the flop as a set-mine, but after missing the turn I should avoid bloating an OOP pot. Check for pot control and to allow Elon to bluff/capture the pot; I have essentially no equity vs most of his continuing ranges and calling a realistic turn bet is -EV. I will only call a ridiculously small bet (≈≤120 chips) that gives correct pot odds to chase one out; otherwise I will fold to any normal-sized bet. Therefore: check now and fold to any meaningful bet on the turn.

Turn board 2♦ adds a diamond flush draw to a coordinated flop (Tc Kh 9d), increasing draws like QJ (str8), Q8, diamond draws, and wheel draws. My A♠5♠ remains ace-high with no pair/draw, but blocks AA/AK/ATs; backdoor spade flush is live but thin. Preflop button raise + flop c-bet reps strong (KK+, AK, KQ, KT, T9s, sets), while Minni's OOP call-check range is capped: weaker Kx (KJ/KT/K9), Tx (T9), 9x, Ax (AJ+/weaker), draws (JQ, Q8). Flash folded short, so heads-up deep vs Minni (14500). Betting turn risks value-owning with ace-high (often behind pairs) or bloating pot OOP for her; thin value from worse Ax unlikely given board. Semi-bluff fold equity low vs her calling station tendencies (history: calls multi-streets deep with marginal hands like in H2, H17). Checking realizes equity (live backdoors), controls pot, denies her free river cards if improving, retains showdown value OOP, and sets up river bluffs/value if check-back. Position allows free river decision; standard to check marginal ace-high after c-bet called OOP on drawy board.

I called preflop as a set‑mine and called a small flop c‑bet. The turn and river checked down: board T♣ K♥ 9♦ 2♦ 5♦. I never improved — I still have an underpair (6s6c) and no diamonds. Elon’s line (small flop bet → check turn → check river) is consistent with one‑pair hands (Kx/Tx/9x/pocket pairs) and some missed Ax/queen‑type bluffs. Most of that checking range beats me at showdown, and a river bluff here has limited fold equity (many of his hands will call a realistic river bluff). Betting would be a pure bluff that risks chips OOP for marginal fold equity; given my stack, position and earlier plan to avoid bloating the pot with air, checking to showdown is the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line.

River 5♦ pairs the board's lowest card but completes the diamond flush (9♦2♦5♦), adding possible flushes (AdXd, KdXd, QdJd, etc.) to villain's capped OOP check-call flop/turn range (weak Kx like KJ/KT/K9, Tx, 9x, Ax mid, missed draws like QJ/OESD). My A♠5♠ improved to bottom pair (5♠5♦) + A kicker + spade flush blocker, but still weak vs likely pairs (Kx/Tx/9x > bottom pair) and any flush/set/straight/two-pair. Preflop raise + flop c-bet called OOP reps strong for me (KK+/AK/KQ/KT/T9s/sets); my turn check reps draw/heard or slowplay, capping my range somewhat. Villain (Minni) checks river OOP after multi-checks, suggesting marginal showdown hand (weak Kx, Ax-high, missed draw) unwilling to bet but capable of calling bets (station-y history: H2 calls huge w/5s pair+flush dr, H17 multi-call Qh3h dr). Pot 2600 deep vs 14500, betting thin value from worse (J8+/worse Ax? unlikely given board/calls) risks value-owning (often behind) or getting called by better; fold equity low vs her calling tendencies. Checking realizes equity (beats bluffs/A-high/draws), controls pot OOP, induces check-behind from her value (extracts 0 EV but safe), and preserves stack for better spots in long 150-hand game. Bluff unprofitable (river flush completion + paired 5♦ reps strength to her, low fold equity). Standard check-behind with showdown value on coordinated paired board vs sticky defender.

I’m on the button with A♣ J♥ — a strong, positionally advantaged hand. With both blinds in I want to take the initiative, pick up the blinds, or isolate the short-stacked SB. A standard 2.5–3x BB sizing (~300 total) gives solid fold equity and keeps weaker hands honest; Flash (SB) has only 1,100 and may shove wide (which I can call comfortably with AJo given my deep stack). Limping/calling surrenders initiative and invites multiway action where AJ is less comfortable, while folding is too weak. I’ll raise to 300 to seize position and build the pot on my terms.

I am holding 7h 3h in the Small Blind. My current stack is very short at only 1,100 chips (11 big blinds). Minni has opened for 3x from the button. While the hand is suited, its high-card value is extremely poor, and playing it out of position with such a shallow stack is not advisable. I need to preserve my remaining chips for a better spot to go all-in or make a move. Calling would commit nearly 30% of my stack to a speculative hand. Therefore, the best play is to fold and wait for a stronger hand.

Q3 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, dominated by any reasonable raising range from the button. Facing a standard raise from Minni, who has been aggressive but positionally strong here, and being out of position postflop with deep stacks, folding is the correct decision to preserve stack and avoid difficult spots. No fold equity needed since SB folded, and calling would require too much equity against their range.

I am on the button with Jd 8h. I have a very short stack of 1100 chips (11 big blinds). Standard play in a three-handed game with this stack size would often be to shove or fold, but given the structure and the need to survive or double up, I will enter the pot by raising to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down pre-flop. If shoved on, I'll have to evaluate, but a small raise allows me some fold equity against weak ranges in the blinds.

K5o is a marginal offsuit king hand with poor post-flop playability, especially out of position in the small blind facing a button raise from Flash, who likely has a wide range. Pot odds to call 250 into 450 offer about 1.8:1, but expected value is negative given low equity against Flash's range and potential multiway pot with Minni in BB. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in this deep-stack cash game to maximize long-term profits over 150 hands.

I’m in the big blind with Qd9h facing a 3BB button open to 300. Pot is 450 and I need to put 200 more to call. Heads-up Q9o is worth defending and the immediate pot odds are favorable to see a flop. Flash is effectively very short (~8BB behind), so a 3-bet just invites an all‑in and a tough fold/call decision; I’d rather keep the pot manageable and realize equity postflop. I’ll call the 200 to see a flop and reassess — if Flash jams over my call I’ll fold most of the time since the required equity to profitably call that shove is too high against a likely shove range.

Flop: Js Jc As. I hold Qd9h — no pair, no backdoor spade/flush, basically zero showdown value vs realistic ranges. Flash is effectively very short (~8BB) so his most profitable play is often to shove to fold me or get called by worse; calling a shove (~800) into a 650 pot requires ~55% equity, which Q9o almost never has vs a shove range that includes Jx/Ax. Betting here only creates a decision where I’ll be forced to fold to or be crushed by a shove. I’ll keep the pot small and realize equity — check. Plan: fold to any sizable shove/bet; only consider calling a tiny non‑all‑in bet (unlikely given stacks).

I have trip Jacks on an Ace-high board (Js Jc As). With only 800 chips remaining and a pot of 650, I am looking to get my stack into the middle. Minni checked to me after calling pre-flop. I will place a bet of 300, which is roughly half the pot, to represent a standard continuation bet and induce a call or a raise from any Ace, pocket pair, or draw in her range. This leaves me with a small commitment for the turn.

Flop Js Jc As — I have Qd9h: no pair, no backdoors and virtually zero showdown value. Flash is very short-stacked and opened preflop, so his continuation bet of 300 into the ~950 pot is not a tiny probe — it’s the kind of sizing that either has J/A value or is a shove‑feasible pressure play. Calling 300 gives only ~24% immediate pot‑odds and he can still shove his remaining ~200, which raises the required equity to ~28%; Q9o has almost no equity vs a realistic Jx/Ax heavy continuation/shove range. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for a better spot rather than hero‑call or build a pot where I’m dominated.

Preflop on the button with 2h5s offsuit, one of the worst possible hands with negligible equity and playability. Pot is 150, to call 100 into it offers poor odds for a speculative hand with no straight or flush potential in most scenarios. Deep stack but no reason to enter the pot with trash; folding preserves stack for better spots. Flash is short-stacked at 1350 but still not worth playing this.

I’m in the small blind with K♥J♥. Elon folded, only the big blind (Flash) remains and has a short stack (~1,350 ≈ 13.5bb). It costs me 50 to see the flop; KJs is a strong heads-up hand that plays well both preflop and postflop and has good equity versus a wide shove range from a short-stacked BB. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the big blind) — standard steal size heads-up that generates fold equity and isolates the short stack. Min-raising to 200 is too small (gives less fold equity); calling 50 is passive. If Flash shoves I’ll be comfortable calling given my equity and our deep overall stack; if he calls we have a playable hand with initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind situation. My stack is quite short at 13.5 big blinds (1350 chips). Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. I have position on Minni for the rest of the hand. While a shove (all-in) is a viable strategy here to maximize fold equity, calling allows me to use my position and see a flop. With an SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) of around 1.75 on the flop if I call, I am essentially committed to any favorable flop (any pair or significant draw), but calling keeps Minni's wider range in the pot, potentially allowing me to extract more value if I hit or to play optimally based on her post-flop action.

I was the preflop aggressor (raised to 300) and we're heads-up vs a very short-stacked BB (~1,150). The flop (3♦ 9♠ 9♦) leaves me with K♥J♥ — no pair and no meaningful draw — so this is a pure pressure/bluff-spot rather than a value hand. A continuation bet is standard: it leverages my preflop raise, folds out many overcards and weak hands that called pre, and forces the short stack into a tough decision. I size to ~2/3 pot (400) to maximize fold equity while keeping my own risk limited; if Flash jams over this bet I will fold KJ because my equity vs a shove-heavy range is below the break-even threshold, and if he simply calls I can proceed cautiously on later streets. Checking is too passive here and concedes the initiative, so I will bet to try to take the pot now.

I am currently playing as the Big Blind with a very short stack of approximately 11.5 big blinds. Pre-flop, I called a raise from the Small Blind (Minni) with Jack-Ten offsuit. The flop of 3d-9s-9d is a paired board that doesn't significantly help my hand, leaving me with only Jack-high. Minni has led out for 400 chips into a 600-chip pot (making the total pot 1000). Calling would require committing more than a third of my remaining stack (400 out of 1150) with a marginal hand that has little equity against a range that likely includes many pocket pairs or even a stray Nine. Given my stack size, I cannot afford to float on this board. Folding allows me to preserve my remaining 11.5 big blinds for a better spot, likely an all-in shove pre-flop in a future hand, rather than bleeding chips on a board where I am likely behind.

Button — open a wide stealing range. 6♥5♦ is a marginal hand but has good post‑flop playability and benefits huge from position, so I want to raise rather than limp. SB is short (1,100) and will often fold or shove; a 3x open to 300 maximizes fold equity and prices out some callers. If the SB shoves I'll call (their range is wide and pot odds are attractive); if the big blind (Elon) 3‑bets large (≈900+), I will fold 6‑5o out of position. Plan postflop: use position to c‑bet favourable boards, fold to heavy resistance.

I am holding 8d 3h in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand. Minni has raised from the Button, and I have a very short stack of only 1100 chips. Calling 250 (more than 20% of my remaining stack) with such a poor hand out of position is a losing strategy. I should fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my chips.

Pocket 7s is a strong hand preflop, especially heads-up against a button raise in a short-handed game. Minni's button opening range is wide due to position and her history of frequent raises. 77 has excellent equity against that range (dominates many pairs, overcards, suited connectors). Calling risks giving her live cards or multiway if Flash wakes up, but he's short and folded. 3-betting isolates, builds the pot when ahead, and takes down blinds frequently. Standard sizing 3x her 300 (to 900 total) balances value and pressure. Deep stacks support this without overcommitting.

Elon 3‑bet to 900 after my 3x open to 300 and the SB folded, so I’m heads‑up. Pot is 1,250 and calling 600 requires ~32% equity (600 / 1,850 ≈ 32.4%). 6♥5♦ offsuit has poor equity and post‑flop playability versus a BB 3‑bet range that will contain many pairs and strong broadways; a light 4‑bet bluff is unjustified with these stacks and villain’s tendencies. Follow my preflop plan: don’t get sticky in a marginal spot — fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 8s 7c and a very short stack of 1100 chips. Standard opening strategies apply less when stack sizes are this lopsided, but 87 offsuit is a marginal hand. However, given my low stack (11 big blinds), I need to find spots to either double up or pick up blinds. In a 3-handed game, 87o is a reasonable hand to open from the button to put pressure on the blinds, but with only 11BB, if I enter the pot, I'm often committed. Given the previous aggressive play from Elon and Minni, a fold is safer here to wait for a better hand to commit my remaining chips.

Jd6c is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, low high-card strength, and bad playability out of position in the SB. Minni in BB defends frequently based on history (calls stations in many pots). No fold equity advantage with this hand, and risking chips OOP post-flop is -EV. Standard play is to fold trash like this and wait for better stealing spots or premium hands. Pot odds not compelling (50 to call into 150, but multiway potential none since Flash folded, still OOP trouble). Preserve deep stack for value spots.

Preflop on the button with pocket 5s (55), a strong medium pair in a multiway limped pot (SB Minni limped for 100 total, BB Flash checked). Position is excellent. Opponents have wide limping/checking ranges including Ax suited, suited connectors, weak pairs, offsuit broadways. Raising builds the pot for value when ahead, protects against overcards, and can isolate the short-stacked BB (10BB) or fold out trash. Standard sizing in limped pot is 3.5-4x BB (350-400). Raising to 400 applies good pressure without overbetting, pot-committing BB somewhat while keeping Minni's deep stack involved if she has a hand. Folding or limping is too passive; 55 plays well postflop from position. History shows Minni/Flash call light but fold to aggression often.

I have 9♠3♦ — a very weak, unsuited hand with almost no playability. Elon opened to 400 from the button; calling would cost me 350 more into a 550 pot (I’ve already posted the 50 SB). That requires about 350/(550+350) ≈ 39% equity to break even, which 9‑3o simply doesn’t have, especially out of position. The big blind is short (1,000) and may come along, creating a multiway pot where my hand fares even worse. 3‑betting as a bluff is a poor idea with this holding. With a healthy 16,950 stack and many hands left to play, I should preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am in the big blind with 9d 6c, which is a weak hand. Elon (the button) has raised to 400. Although the pot odds are roughly 2.8:1, my stack is extremely short at only 1000 chips (10 big blinds). Defending such a weak hand out of position with a shallow stack is usually a losing play. I'm better off preserving my remaining chips for a better opportunity to double up or shove.

Button in a 3-handed pot with J♦8♣ (pot 150, 100 to call). In 3-handed play my opening range should be wider and this is a reasonable steal attempt. Flash (SB) is a very short stack (~950) so a standard 3xBB open to 300 gives good fold equity and either takes the blinds or isolates the short stack. Plan: raise to 300; if Flash shoves and Elon folds I'll call (pot odds ~32.5%); if Flash shoves and Elon also calls, or if Elon 3-bets big (~900), I will fold J8o to avoid a poor multiway/squeezed spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s2c, a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. My stack is extremely short at only 950 chips (less than 10 Big Blinds), while Minni and Elon have massive stacks. Entering a pot out of position with a junk hand against large stacks is a losing play. I should preserve my remaining chips and look for a better spot to commit my stack.

Pocket fives is a premium hand for set-mining with excellent implied odds due to deep stacks (219 BB effective with Minni). Button raise after SB fold suggests a wide range including Ax, suited connectors, broadway. 55 has strong equity (~45-55% vs. typical BTN open range) and crushes many calling hands. Flat calling offers good pot odds (200 into 450, ~2.25:1, needing ~30% equity) but OOP post-flop. Prefer 3-betting to ~3x (900 total) to deny equity to worse hands like KJo, ATo, build pot for sets, and take initiative. History shows Minni raises frequently from button but folds to aggression often (e.g., Hands 15,19,47). If 4-bet, can fold profitably as preflop loss small. Raising aligns with aggressive BB defense strategy to maximize stack long-term.

Elon’s 3‑bet to 900 after my 3x open makes the pot 1,250 and I have to call 600 (pot odds ≈ 32%). J♦8♣ offsuit is a marginal hand that is often dominated by a typical 3‑bet range (AQ+, KQ, mid pairs, suited broadways). Even though I’d be in position, J8o’s equity vs a polarized 3‑bet range is below the required break‑even and it’s a poor candidate for a 4‑bet bluff or calling to play a big pot out of line. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot—fold.

I am on the button with 9c 7h and a very short stack of only 950 chips (9.5 big blinds). Looking at my stack size relative to the blinds (50/100), I am in a 'push or fold' situation. 97o is not a strong enough hand to jam for 9.5 BBs from the button against two active opponents, especially since Elon and Minni have massive stacks and might call lighter to knock me out. Raising small is also not an option as it commits too much of my stack. I will fold and wait for a better spot to put my remaining chips in.

Pocket kings is one of the best starting hands in NLHE. With Flash folded on the button, the pot is unraised, and only Minni in the big blind remains. I should raise to build the pot for value, isolate against the BB, and take initiative. Standard raise size is 3x the big blind (300 total), as stacks are deep and this is a premium hand. Raising from the small blind OOP is standard despite position disadvantage, given hand strength. Minni has been aggressive historically but KK crushes their calling range.

Fold. Tc3d is a weak, poorly-connected offsuit hand and I’m out of position postflop facing a 300 open from the SB. I must call 200 more into a 400 pot, so I need ~33% equity to break even. T3o is usually well under that vs a standard SB open and will be dominated and hard to play for multiple streets. A min 3-bet bluff is possible but high-variance against Elon’s stack and opening frequency; conserving chips for better spots is the +EV play here.

Preflop on the button in a 3-handed pot with JTo offsuit. Pot is 150, to call 100, offering about 2.5:1 odds, but position is excellent. Likely SB (Minni) limped, BB (Flash) has a tiny stack of 850 (~8.5BB), making this a great spot to raise for value/isolation or to steal dead money. JTo offsuit plays well postflop against shortstack shoving ranges and calling ranges from limpers in position. Standard raise size to 3x the BB (300 total). History shows loose play from opponents, supporting aggression here to build stack long-term.

Elon opened from the button to 300 and I’m in the small blind with K♥7♣. This is a marginal, easily-dominated Kx hand and I’ll be out of position for the entire hand. Calling 250 to play OOP against an aggressive button and a very short-stacked big blind (who can shove and force awkward decisions) is a low-ROI spot. A minimum 3-bet to 500 is just a light bluff that still invites a call/4-bet from Elon or a shove from Flash; I don’t want to manufacture a marginal pot out of position with this holding. With a deep stack and plenty of hands left, folding and conserving chips for better spots is the best long-term play.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in poker. Although I am short-stacked with only 8.5 big blinds and the pot odds are somewhat attractive to call the raise, committing nearly 25% of my remaining stack to see a flop out of position with this hand is not strategically sound. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my chips.

Button with Ac4h — this is a hand worth opening in position. Pot is 150, to-call 100. I want to apply pressure to the short-stacked SB and use position versus the BB, so I’ll open-raise to 300 (standard 3x BB sizing). Raising here (vs simply calling) wins the blinds more often, isolates the short stack, and gives better pot-odds if Flash shoves. If Flash (800) shoves I’m willing to call given I have an ace and decent equity against a wide short-stack shove; if Elon (deep) gives a sizable 3-bet I will fold A4o and preserve my deeper stack. If called, I’ll play postflop in position — c-bet thin on safe boards and proceed cautiously on coordinated/heavy heart boards.

I am currently very short-stacked with only 8 big blinds (800 chips). In a 3-handed game, K4 suited is a hand that performs reasonably well against a wide button opening range. With such a shallow stack, playing post-flop out of position is not viable, so my strategy must be push-or-fold. Shoving here maximizes my fold equity against Minni and potentially Elon in the big blind, while ensuring I realize my equity if called.

Qd2s is a complete trash hand: offsuit, unconnected, low cards with poor playability. Facing an open-raise from Minni on the button followed by a 3-bet from Flash in the small blind, the range for both is strong. I'm out of position in the big blind with a deep stack, but calling 700 into a 1200 pot gives implied odds that don't justify it given the hand's lack of equity or postflop potential. Raising would require too wide a range and burn money; folding preserves stack for better spots.

I opened to 300 on the button with Ac4h and Flash (SB) 3‑bet to 800. Elon folded. Flash is acting like a short, wide 3‑bettor here, and I have position plus an ace — enough equity to continue but not a hand I want to 4‑bet/shove with. Calling keeps the pot controllable, isolates the small blind, and lets me realize positional advantage post‑flop. A fold would be too conservative given my open and his likely wide range; a raise/shove is unnecessary against a short stack and would risk more marginal spots. Plan postflop: c‑bet A‑high or dry boards, proceed cautiously on coordinated/heart‑heavy runouts, and fold to heavy turn/river aggression when I don’t improve.

With only 50 chips remaining in my stack and a pot of 1700, I am priced in to commit the rest of my chips regardless of my hand strength. I have King-high with a backdoor flush draw on a board of 3c-3d-Jc. Given the massive pot odds, checking is an option, but betting my final chips is the standard way to realize my remaining equity.

Flop is a paired 3-3 with Jc (3c 3d Jc). I have Ac4h — just Ace-high but with a useful overcard and backdoor club possibilities. Flash is a short, wide 3‑bettor and has made a tiny probe bet (50 into a 1750 pot). Calling 50 is essentially free: pot odds are trivial (~2.8%) and calling keeps the pot small while using position to realize equity and extract value if I hit an Ace. Raising would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and folding would be overly tight given the price. Plan: call, reassess on the turn — fire/raise if I pair my Ace or if the turn is very favorable; fold or release to heavy turn/river aggression if I don’t improve and he shows real strength.

9c2d is one of the worst possible hands heads-up from the SB/button position. While heads-up ranges are wide, this hand has terrible playability, low equity against a typical BB defending range (which includes many better hands), and I'd be OOP postflop. Pot odds are favorable for a limp (3:1), but implied odds are poor due to reverse domination and difficulty realizing equity. Raising risks playing OOP with a weak hand after Minni defends or 3-bets. Deep stacks (225BB eff), so preserve stack for premium spots. Folding dead money blinds is standard with trash; wait for 78+ hands left.

Heads‑up on the button with K♠6♥ — this is a clear open-raise spot. Against one opponent I should be opening a wide range and using position to apply pressure. I’ll raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) — big enough to generate fold equity and discourage routine 3-bets, but not so large as to bloat the pot out of position against a very aggressive 3-bettor. Plan: if Elon folds I take the pot; if he calls I play postflop in position and c-bet favorable textures, looking to fold to a large turn aggression if I miss; if he 3-bets smallish (reasonable sizing) I’ll consider a call and play postflop given deep stacks, but I’ll fold to a very large 3-bet to avoid marginal spots.
